name: Hurricane Ian
headline: Florida's costliest hurricane made significantly wetter by climate change

quote.text: There is nothing left. The whole neighborhood is gone. I've never seen anything like this in my life.

attribution.method_note: Storyline method measures magnitude increase, not probability ratio. It asks: given that this storm occurred, how much worse did climate change make it? This is different from the Fraction of Attributable Risk method used for flood and heatwave events.
attribution.confidence_note: Peer-reviewed and published in IOP Science Environmental Research: Climate. Finding that rainfall increase exceeds Clausius-Clapeyron scaling is robust across multiple hurricane studies.
attribution.summary: Climate change made Hurricane Ian's extreme rainfall approximately 18% heavier — far exceeding the 5-7% increase that basic thermodynamics would predict. A separate Climate Central analysis found Ian was one of three Atlantic hurricanes (2019-2023) that strengthened to Category 5 specifically because of climate-warmed ocean temperatures.

method_explainer.what_attribution_science_is: For hurricanes, attribution science uses a storyline approach: scientists run the actual storm track through climate models set to today's conditions and to pre-industrial conditions, then compare rainfall totals. This answers the question 'given this storm, how much worse did climate change make it?' — rather than asking how likely the storm was.
method_explainer.how_this_event_was_studied: Reed and Wehner ran Hurricane Ian's observed track through an ensemble of high-resolution weather model simulations, initialised on 27 September 2022. They compared rainfall accumulations in the factual world against a counterfactual world with pre-industrial sea surface temperatures and atmospheric conditions. The finding — 18% more rainfall — exceeds what Clausius-Clapeyron thermodynamics would predict, suggesting storm dynamics amplify the climate signal.
method_explainer.what_confidence_means: High confidence because the study is peer-reviewed and published, the methodology has been validated across multiple prior hurricanes including Harvey, and the finding is consistent with the direction predicted by climate physics. The 18% is a central estimate; the real-time estimate published during the storm was a more conservative 10%.

human_impact.people_affected_note: No single verified aggregate figure. Southwest Florida communities across Lee, Charlotte, Sarasota and DeSoto counties severely affected.
human_impact.deaths_note: Direct deaths from storm. Total death toll including indirect causes estimated at 150+ per Florida Medical Examiners Commission.
human_impact.displaced_note: Estimated from power outages and damage reports. Over 2.5 million lost power.
human_impact.economic_loss_note: $110B insured losses per NOAA NCEI. One of the costliest US hurricanes on record.
human_impact.economic_loss_source: NOAA NCEI Billion-Dollar Weather and Climate Disasters
human_impact.emitters_share_source: US share of global cumulative CO2 emissions, Our World in Data

satellite.description: Sentinel-1 SAR showing storm surge flood extent across Southwest Florida before and after landfall

at_risk.context: Lee County alone has one of the highest concentrations of mobile homes in the US. These structures offer little protection against Category 4 winds and storm surge. Many residents did not or could not evacuate.
Florida's property insurance crisis had left many homeowners without adequate coverage before Ian struck. Many are still in legal disputes over claim payments years later.
Pine Island, Fort Myers Beach and Sanibel Island — communities whose economies depend entirely on fishing and tourism — were effectively destroyed. Recovery has been uneven.

stats.label: more rainfall from major Gulf hurricanes by 2100
stats.delta: under high emissions scenario, NOAA projections

stats.label: more likely for storms to rapidly intensify near landfall
stats.delta: as Gulf of Mexico warming accelerates

stats.label: sea level rise in Southwest Florida by 2050
stats.delta: amplifying storm surge from future storms

key_outcomes.description: FEMA approved over $1 billion in individual assistance for Ian survivors, making it one of the largest individual assistance programs in FEMA history. However, many low-income and uninsured residents reported difficulties navigating the claims process.
key_outcomes.status_note: Funds disbursed but access inequities documented by housing advocates.

key_outcomes.description: Florida's property insurance market, already in crisis before Ian, saw multiple insurers become insolvent in 2023. The state legislature passed emergency insurance reforms in December 2022, though their effectiveness remains contested.
Thousands of disputed insurance claims led to litigation surge in Florida courts. Policyholders reported widespread claim denials and underpayments from insurers citing wind vs water damage disputes.

response.accountability_summary: Florida's insurance market collapsed after Ian. Many residents are still uninsured or in legal disputes. The storm accelerated a crisis that existed before it arrived.

curated_coverage.headline: Two years after Ian, Fort Myers Beach is still rebuilding — but who gets to come back?
Ian survivors still fighting insurance companies as storm anniversary approaches
The insurance industry helped create Florida's climate risk — now it's abandoning the state
Hurricane Ian made 18% wetter by climate change, peer-reviewed study finds
